name: Felix Lee
title: Co-founder and CEO
bio:
Felix is the Co-founder & CEO of ADPList and sets the vision and strategy for the company. Founded in 2021, the company grew into a global platform on a mission to help every person unlock their human potential. With 30K+ Mentors from companies like Netflix, Airbnb, and Google, hosting over 100 million minutes of sessions on topics such as product design, product management, and coding in 140+ countries.

As a designer, Felix has embedded his creative roots in ADPList's culture, product, and community.

Featured on Forbes 30 under 30 Asia List and Tatler's Generation T Future Leader List in 2023. In 2021, ADPList raised $1.3 million from Sequoia Capital India and Goodwater Capital.

knowledge_chunks.content: When designing a user experience, always start with user empathy. Conduct interviews or surveys to understand their pain points deeply. This will not only guide your design decisions but also help you create solutions that resonate with users and drive engagement.

Building a community around your product is essential for growth. Focus on creating value for your users through educational content and interaction. Engage with your community regularly, listen to their feedback, and iterate your product based on their needs—this will foster loyalty and advocacy.

Leverage growth hacking techniques by experimenting with different content formats. For instance, if you're focused on LinkedIn Ads, create short video snippets that highlight your product's benefits. Analyze the performance metrics and double down on what resonates the most with your audience.

Effective communication is key in product design. Always present your ideas visually, using tools like Figma to create prototypes. This not only helps in conveying your vision clearly but also facilitates constructive feedback from stakeholders early in the process.

When working on UI design, leverage tools like Amplitude to track user interactions. Understanding how users navigate through your interface can highlight areas that need refinement. Use this data to iterate on your designs, ensuring they align with user behavior.

As a generalist product manager, it's crucial to wear multiple hats. Embrace cross-functional collaboration by actively involving design, marketing, and development teams in the early stages of product development. This holistic approach leads to more innovative solutions and a smoother execution.

In content creation, always align your messaging with your product strategy. Identify key themes that resonate with your target audience and create content that addresses these themes. Consistency in messaging will strengthen your brand identity and help in positioning your product effectively.

When using Twitter Ads for growth, focus on creating compelling visuals and concise messaging. Test different ad formats and audience segments to find what works best. Don't forget to track your performance metrics, as this will help you optimize your campaigns for maximum impact.

name: Sarah Chen
title: Senior Product Manager
bio: 8 years in product across Google, Stripe, and two YC startups. I specialize in helping early-career folks break into PM roles and navigate career transitions.

knowledge_chunks.content: When switching careers, always identify your transferable skills first. Don't focus on what you lack — focus on what you bring that others in the new field don't have. Your outsider perspective is often your biggest asset. Map every single skill you have to the new domain before you even apply anywhere. Your past experience is never wasted, it's just repositioned.

Never give a number first. Always anchor high with a range, and make sure the bottom of your range is your actual target number. When they make an offer, pause before responding — silence is a powerful negotiation tool. Most people leave 10-20% on the table by accepting the first offer. Always negotiate total comp: equity, bonus, remote flexibility all have real dollar value.

The biggest mistake I see is candidates who talk about what they did, not the impact they created. Interviewers don't remember tasks — they remember outcomes. Every answer should follow: situation, action, result, and why it mattered to the business. For PM interviews specifically, always tie your answer back to user value and business metrics.

New managers often try to solve problems their team should solve. Your job shifts from doing to enabling. The best thing I learned: hold weekly 1:1s where you ask 'What's blocking you?' and then actually remove those blockers. Stop being the smartest person in the room — start being the person who makes everyone else smarter.

Prioritization is the core PM skill nobody teaches properly. I use impact vs effort, but more importantly I always ask: what would we regret NOT doing in 6 months? And always be able to say clearly why you're NOT building something — that's as important as what you are building.

name: Boris Yanovskiy
title: Principal Data and Business Analyst
bio:
I am an experienced Business Analyst, Data Analyst, and Project Manager with over 20 years of expertise in Capital Markets, Wealth Management, Lending, and Fixed Income. As a certified Scrum Master, I have successfully led initiatives in data governance, regulatory reporting, and compliance at leading financial institutions such as Wells Fargo and Bank of America.

Proficient in Data Visualization, Hadoop, and Oracle, I thrive in both Agile and Waterfall environments. I excel at transforming complex business requirements into practical technology solutions, fostering innovation and operational excellence.

Known for my effective communication and strategic thinking, I have a strong track record in stakeholder management. I am committed to enhancing data analysis and project management practices to help organizations achieve their goals.

knowledge_chunks.content: Effective stakeholder management hinges on proactive communication. Always schedule regular check-ins to gauge their needs and expectations. Use tools like Confluence to document discussions and decisions, ensuring everyone is aligned and informed.

When working with SQL, focus on writing clean and efficient queries. Aim to optimize your data retrieval processes, especially when dealing with large datasets. Additionally, leverage Tableau to visualize your findings—this will help communicate complex data insights in a more digestible format to stakeholders.

In Agile environments, ensure that your user stories are well-defined. Collaborate closely with your team to break down tasks into manageable units. Regularly review and adapt your sprint goals based on team feedback and stakeholder input to drive continuous improvement.

Utilize Databricks to streamline your data processing workflows. Encourage your team to collaborate in real-time within the platform, sharing insights and challenges as they arise. This fosters a culture of innovation and helps identify solutions faster.

When using Balsamiq for wireframing, prioritize user experience in your designs. Involve your team in the process—gather feedback early and often. This collaborative approach not only improves the end product but also strengthens team dynamics.

As a leader, address conflicts head-on with empathy and transparency. Facilitate open discussions where team members can express their concerns. This not only resolves issues but also builds trust and encourages a more cohesive team environment.

Effective time management is crucial in balancing stakeholder demands. Prioritize tasks based on urgency and impact, and use tools like Jira to track progress. Communicate timelines clearly with stakeholders to set realistic expectations.

Leverage business intelligence tools to analyze trends and derive actionable insights. When faced with a complex problem, break it down into smaller components and tackle them systematically. This structured approach will help you identify effective solutions more efficiently.

name: Andreas Grunewald
title: Founder / Head of Product
bio: With over 15 years of experience in product management, software engineering and development, I bring a wealth of knowledge and expertise. My passion for mentoring, coaching, and fostering team growth with kindness and emotional intelligence is a testament to my commitment to professional development. I have successfully led and organized cross-functional, agile teams that are focused, well-organized, and high-performing. My engagement and accountability extend to working with internal and external stakeholders, developers, directors, and the C-suite—my detail-oriented and data-driven approach results in outcome-focused and value-driven product roadmaps. My broad experience includes building modern SaaS web applications for internal and external clients, including consumers, small businesses, and enterprises. Additionally, I have extensive experience in software engineering, architecture, processes, QA, and UX paradigms.

knowledge_chunks.content: When developing your product strategy, ensure you involve stakeholders from the beginning. Schedule regular check-ins to gather feedback and insights, which will help align your roadmap with their expectations. This not only fosters collaboration but also enhances buy-in, making it easier to navigate potential roadblocks later.

Use data analytics to inform your product decisions. Set up dashboards with key performance indicators that can be easily monitored. This allows you to pivot quickly in response to user behavior and market trends, ensuring that your product remains relevant and effective.

In an agile environment, communication is key. Implement daily stand-ups to keep your team aligned and accountable. Encourage open discussions about challenges and successes, so everyone feels empowered to contribute to the project's success.

When integrating generative AI into your product, start with a clear understanding of the user needs it will address. Develop a prototype that demonstrates its capabilities and gather user feedback early. This iterative approach helps refine the product and ensures it adds real value.

Conduct user research not just at the beginning of your project, but continuously throughout the development cycle. Use tools like surveys and usability tests to gather insights. This ongoing engagement will help you make informed design choices that resonate with your users.

Prototyping is a powerful tool for collaboration. Use platforms like Figma to create interactive prototypes that your team and stakeholders can explore. This not only visualizes your ideas but also facilitates constructive feedback, leading to a more refined final product.

In your content creation strategy, prioritize SEO from the outset. Use keyword research tools to identify terms that resonate with your target audience. By integrating these keywords into your content, you can enhance visibility and drive more organic traffic to your product.

As a leader, practice empathy by actively listening to your team members. Create a safe space for them to share their thoughts and concerns. This approach not only builds trust but also cultivates a strong team culture that can drive innovation and productivity.
